title: Projecting household and living arrangements in Asia
abstract: Households are experiencing substantial transformations nowadays. In many societies of the world, household size is steadily declining while the number of households is increasing at a substantially faster rate than that of population growth. In Germany and Norway, for example, about one third of the households comprises of solo-livers (i.e., those living alone). In China, one-person households will more than double from 58 million in 2010 to 126 million in 2050. Among the many issues related to ongoing household changes, the living arrangement of older adults is a main challenge in view of the unprecedented population ageing. As households become smaller, who will take care of the older generation? Changes in elderly living arrangements are key issues driving the demand for longterm care. It is important to note that these household changes have huge impacts on energy consumption, environment, and sustainable development. Water, electricity, gas, vehicles, appliances, furniture, banking/financing, and many other resources, commodities, and services are often purchased and consumed by households rather than individuals. The smaller household size, in this sense, has significant implications for the world’s sustainable future, as it is likely to lead to higher per capita resource consumption. In the light of such concerns, it has been recently recommended that the household should be prioritised in research and practice in planning for sustainable development (Gu, Feng, Wang, & Zeng, 2015). Projections of household and living arrangements are therefore important research topics that are drawing increasing interest in academic research, policymaking and market analyses. Relying solely on traditional population projections can be misleading. For example, future years’ energy consumption would be seriously underestimated if the forecasting is only based on population growth, which is slowing down and will soon become negative in many countries. Figure 1 compares population-based and household-based projections on future residential energy demands in the Hebei province of China (Zeng, Li, & Wang, 2017). As can be seen, the population-based forecasts severely under-estimates future home-based energy demands. This is because while the population growth in Hebei is currently slow and will become negative around 2035, the number of households, which truly determines home-based energy demands, will continue to increase substantially in the next four decades. In other words, householdbased projections provide more realistic scenarios of future energy demands than traditional population-based projections.
